content.text: Before transformers dominated language AI,
convolutional neural networks (CNNs)
and
recurrent neural networks (RNNs)

were the primary architectures for vision and sequential text processing. Modern LLM agents rarely use RNNs for core language generation, but CNNs remain relevant for vision inputs, and understanding RNN limitations explains why attention-based models won.
What it is

CNNs apply small learned filters across spatial grids (images, spectrograms) to detect local patterns hierarchically — edges, textures, objects. RNNs process sequences step by step, maintaining a hidden state that summarizes past inputs. Variants like LSTM and GRU address vanishing gradients for longer sequences. Both are feed-forward or recurrent neural architectures predating the transformer era.
Why it matters

Agent builders encounter CNNs when processing screenshots, UI elements, camera feeds, or document images in multimodal agents. RNN concepts (sequential state, vanishing memory) explain historical NLP approaches and clarify why transformers replaced RNNs for long-context language tasks. Knowing the overview prevents confusion when reading older papers or hybrid architectures.
Benefits
CNNs excel at local patterns
— efficient parameter sharing via convolutions
RNNs model order naturally
— each step conditions on prior hidden state
Mature tooling
— well-supported in PyTorch, TensorFlow, and edge runtimes
Smaller models for narrow tasks
— lightweight CNN/RNN models still useful for classification on device
Trade-offs
RNNs struggle with very long sequences due to vanishing/exploding gradients and sequential computation
RNN inference cannot be fully parallelized over time steps — slow for long texts
CNNs are not designed for arbitrary-length discrete token sequences without adaptation
Neither architecture matches transformer scale efficiency for billion-parameter language modeling
How it works
A CNN layer slides a kernel over input patches. Each output cell aggregates a local neighborhood through the kernel weights and an activation function. Stacked layers build hierarchical features.
An RNN updates hidden state h_t at each timestep t using input x_t and previous state h_{t-1}:
h_t = activation(W_xh * x_t + W_hh * h_{t-1} + b)
LSTM adds gating mechanisms (input, forget, output gates) to control information flow, allowing longer memory than vanilla RNNs.
When to use it

Use CNNs (or CNN-based vision encoders) when agents process images, video frames, or spatial data. Use RNNs only for legacy systems or specialized small-sequence tasks — prefer transformers for language. Do not build new text-generation agents on RNNs.
Examples

A multimodal agent passes a screenshot through a CNN-based vision encoder (or a ViT, which uses attention but retains spatial patch concepts) to produce image embeddings fused with text tokens. An older chatbot might have used an LSTM encoder-decoder for short replies; modern agents replace this with transformer decoders.
Real-world example

A warehouse inspection agent uses a lightweight CNN classifier (MobileNet backbone) to detect package damage from camera images in 40ms on edge hardware. The text reasoning layer is an LLM; the vision front-end remains convolutional because it is fast, proven, and small enough for on-device deployment.
Common mistakes
Choosing RNNs for new long-context language agent pipelines
Ignoring that modern vision-language models may use ViT (attention) rather than pure CNNs
Assuming CNN/RNN knowledge replaces understanding transformers for LLM work
Best practices
Treat CNN/RNN knowledge as foundational context, not primary agent architecture
Use established pretrained vision backbones rather than training CNNs from scratch
When reading legacy NLP code, map LSTM encoder patterns to transformer encoder equivalents
Comparison with alternatives

Transformers replace sequential RNN processing with parallel self-attention over all positions, enabling better long-range dependencies and GPU-friendly training. CNNs remain strong for grid-structured data; transformers dominate variable-length language. Hybrid models (CNN feature extractor + transformer head) appear in some multimodal systems.
Mental model
CNNs are sliding-pattern detectors over grids. RNNs are a chain of memory cells reading one token at a time. Transformers are a room where every token can look at every other token at once.
Key takeaways
CNNs detect local spatial patterns; RNNs process sequences with hidden state
Transformers replaced RNNs for large-scale language modeling due to parallelism and long-range attention
Agents still encounter CNNs in vision pipelines and multimodal inputs
Learn CNN/RNN concepts for context, build new language agents on transformers
